nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; generally found from the surface to about 20–30 m in coral-rich lagoons and semi-protected seaward reefs, closely associated with live Pocillopora and other small-polyped stony corals on which it feeds

decorPreferences: live rock with numerous hiding places
abundant living small-polyped stony corals (Pocillopora) as a food source

diet.notes: Obligate corallivore. In the wild, has been documented to feed almost exclusively on live coral polyps, particularly of the genus Pocillopora and other small-polyped stony corals. In captivity, this specialised diet is the defining husbandry challenge: most individuals refuse all prepared and frozen foods and slowly starve unless provided with a continuous supply of living coral colonies. A few specimens have been reported to accept enriched frozen preparations (mysid shrimp, soaked formulas, sponge-based foods), but this is the exception rather than the rule. Generally regarded as not suitable for the home aquarium for this reason.

avoidWith: conspecifics outside a bonded male-female pair

summary: An Indian Ocean butterflyfish with a pale body crossed by fine dark pinstripes and a yellow-and-orange posterior. An obligate coral-polyp feeder, it is generally considered expert-only and unsuitable for most home aquariums.
careNotes:
The Melon Butterflyfish (Chaetodon trifasciatus) is widely regarded as one of the most difficult Chaetodon species to keep, and the major aquarium-trade sources (LiveAquaria, ReefApp) both flag it as expert-only or 'not suitable for the home aquarium'. The reason is dietary: the species has been documented in the wild as an obligate corallivore, feeding almost exclusively on live coral polyps of the genus Pocillopora and other small-polyped stony corals. Most captive specimens refuse prepared and frozen foods and slowly starve. A small minority of individuals have been reported to learn to accept enriched mysis, sponge-based preparations, or coral-based formulas, but this is unreliable and should not be assumed.

It is not reef-safe: aquarium-trade sources report nibbling on SPS coral polyps, Tridacna clam mantles, anemones, and tubeworms — essentially the same items it would feed on in the wild.

Behaviourally, the species is generally considered to form lifelong monogamous pairs in the wild, with adults swimming and feeding together over a defended reef territory. It is reported as territorial and aggressive toward conspecifics outside the bonded pair, and may also harass other Chaetodon species in a shared tank. If a pair is attempted, both fish should be introduced simultaneously.

Cross-source summary: Adult size is consistently reported at ~15 cm (LiveAquaria 6 in / 15 cm; FishBase 15.0 cm TL; ReefApp 15 cm; Wikipedia 15 cm). Water-temperature and pH details were absent from LiveAquaria and ReefApp; the values used here are the conventional Indo-Pacific reef ranges (24–27°C; pH 8.1–8.4; SG 1.020–1.025) consistent with FishBase's tropical reef classification (depth 0–30 m, 30°N–30°S). Tank-size recommendations diverged: LiveAquaria 75 gal (~284 L) vs ReefApp 300 L; the larger value was selected and rounded to 300 L from the allowed-values list. No reliable lifespan data was located across the sources consulted.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Order Acanthuriformes, Family Chaetodontidae), maximum length (15.0 cm TL), depth range (0–30 m), Indian-Ocean native range (East Africa to western Indonesia, Andaman Sea, Cocos-Keeling, Christmas Island), IUCN status (Least Concern), monogamous pair-bonding, territorial behaviour, and the obligate-corallivore feeding ecology (Pocillopora).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm), minimum tank volume (300 L), reef-safety (not reef safe; feeds on coral polyps, Tridacna clams, tubeworms), specialised SPS-polyp diet that 'will not survive on a replacement food', and overall verdict that the species is 'not suitable for home aquarium'. Used in tank-size disagreement averaging (300 L vs LiveAquaria's ~284 L → 300 L).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (subgenus Corallochaetodon, described M. Park 1797), monogamous adult pair-bonding, territorial behaviour toward other Chaetodon, secretive juvenile phase within coral structures, and Indian-Ocean range (East Africa to Western Java).
